rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015R0401 - Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401 of 25 February 2015, which amends Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005 on maximum residue levels of pesticides.
rows.actualResponse:
The search returned a directly relevant result. Here is the answer:

---

## EU Act Setting MRLs for Acetamiprid and Tebuconazole

### The Key Instrument: Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401

The EU act you are looking for is **Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401** (CELEX: **32015R0401**), adopted on **25 February 2015**. Its full title is:

> *"Amending Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005 of the European Parliament and of the Council as regards maximum residue levels for acetamiprid, chromafenozide, cyazofamid, dicamba, difenoconazole, fenpyrazamine, fluazinam, formetanate, nicotine, penconazole, pymetrozine, pyraclostrobin, tau-fluvalinate and tebuconazole in or on certain products."*

---

### What the Regulation Does

- **Amends Annexes II and III** of the parent Regulation **(EC) No 396/2005** — the core EU framework on maximum residue levels (MRLs) of pesticides in or on food and feed of plant and animal origin — by setting or modifying specific MRLs for a list of active substances including **acetamiprid** and **tebuconazole**. [start_cite]32015R0401[end_cite]

- **For acetamiprid**: MRLs were previously set in Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005. This Regulation modifies those MRLs, specifically in relation to **apricots and tree nuts**, following a request based on authorised uses in the United States that led to residues exceeding existing EU limits, necessitating higher MRLs to avoid trade barriers. [start_cite]32015R0401[end_cite]

- **For tebuconazole**: MRLs were also previously set in Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005. This Regulation amends them specifically in relation to **poppy seed**, following an application for modification. [start_cite]32015R0401[end_cite]

---

### Scientific Basis

The European Food Safety Authority (**EFSA**) assessed all applications and concluded that — for all modified MRLs (excluding certain cases such as fenpyrazamine on peaches and tau-fluvalinate on specific crops) — the modifications were **acceptable with regard to consumer safety**, based on a consumer exposure assessment for **27 specific European consumer groups**. Neither the lifetime exposure via all food products, nor short-term exposure from high consumption of relevant crops, showed a risk that the **acceptable daily intake (ADI)** or the **acute reference dose (ARfD)** would be exceeded. [start_cite]32015R0401[end_cite]

---

### Application Date

The Regulation entered into force the day after its publication and **applies from 1 November 2014** (with a special date of **14 August 2014** for the MRL for formetanate in strawberries). [start_cite]32015R0401[end_cite]

---

|---|---|---|
| **32015R0401** | COMMISSION REGULATION (EU) 2015/401 | Directly sets MRLs for acetamiprid and tebuconazole in or on certain products by amending Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005. |

rows.question: Which EU regulation amended the rules on the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32012R1028 - Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 October 2012, amending Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 as regards the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers.
rows.actualResponse:
The document that directly and precisely answers your question is **Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012** (CELEX: **32012R1028**). Here is a detailed summary:

---

## 📋 Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 — Single Payment Scheme & Vine-Growers

**Full Title:** *Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 October 2012 amending Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 as regards the regime of the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers*

**Policy Domain:** Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries
**Year:** 2012 | **Year Band:** 2010–2014
**Document Type:** Regulation

---

### 🔍 Background & Purpose

The regulation amended **Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007** (the "Single CMO Regulation"), which had established the common organisation of agricultural markets. [start_cite]32012R1028[end_cite]

Under the original Article 103o of that regulation, **Member States could grant decoupled aid under the single payment scheme to vine-growers**. However, this mechanism created practical problems: [start_cite]32012R1028[end_cite]

- Member States could modify transfers to the single payment scheme only **once a year**.
- Support programmes had a **five-year duration**, while payment entitlements under the single payment scheme were granted for an **indeterminate period**.
- This mismatch resulted in **administrative and budgetary burdens**.

---

### ⚙️ Key Amendments Made

The regulation introduced the following changes to Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007: [start_cite]32012R1028[end_cite]

1. **New paragraph in Article 103n:** Member States were permitted — by **1 August 2013** — to decide to **permanently reduce** the amounts available for wine sector support programmes from 2015 onwards, in order to increase their national ceilings for direct payments. Once transferred, those funds would definitively remain in the national ceilings for direct payments and would no longer be available for wine sector support measures.

2. **Replacement of Article 103o (Single payment scheme and support to vine-growers):**
   - Member States could decide, **by 1 December 2012**, to continue providing support to vine-growers for the year **2014** by allocating payment entitlements under the single payment scheme.
   - If the support amount for 2014 exceeded that for 2013, the difference had to be used to allocate new payment entitlements to vine-growers.
   - Support provided for 2014 would **remain in the single payment scheme** and would no longer be available for wine sector measures listed in Articles 103p to 103y.
   - It would also **proportionately reduce** the funds available for those measures in the support programmes.

---

### ✅ Objective

The overarching aim was to **simplify the management** of this specific support measure and ensure its **consistency with the objectives of direct support schemes for farmers**, by converting the mechanism into a straightforward option for Member States to definitively shift funds from wine sector support programmes into their national direct payment ceilings. [start_cite]32012R1028[end_cite]

---

|---|---|---|
| **32012R1028** | REGULATION (EU) No 1028/2012 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL | Directly amends the rules on the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers in Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 |

What does the 2015 EU Decision concerning restrictive measures in view of the situation in Libya change?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2015
- Document type: Decision

rows.actualResponse:
The relevant document was retrieved. Here is a comprehensive breakdown of what **Council Decision (CFSP) 2015/382** changes:

---

# Council Decision (CFSP) 2015/382 — Restrictive Measures Regarding Libya

**CELEX ID:** 32015D0382
**Adopted:** 6 March 2015
**Legal basis:** Article 29 of the Treaty on European Union

This Decision amends the pre-existing **Decision 2011/137/CFSP**, which constitutes the EU's core sanctions framework concerning the situation in Libya. [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

---

## 1. 🔴 Expanded Scope of the Travel Ban (Article 5)

A **new point (c) is added to Article 5(1)** of Decision 2011/137/CFSP, extending the **travel ban** to cover persons **not** already listed in Annex I. The travel ban now applies to persons who engage in or provide support for any of the following: [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

- **Planning, directing, or committing acts** that violate applicable international human rights law or international humanitarian law, or acts constituting **human rights abuses** in Libya
- **Attacks** against any air, land, or sea port in Libya, against a Libyan State institution or installation, or against any **foreign mission** in Libya
- Providing **support for armed groups or criminal networks** through the illicit exploitation of crude oil or any other **natural resources** in Libya
- **Acting on behalf of or at the direction** of persons or entities already listed in Annex II to Decision 2011/137/CFSP

---

## 2. 🔴 Expanded Scope of the Asset Freeze (Article 6)

A **new point (c) is added to Article 6(1)**, extending the **asset freeze** to persons and entities **not** covered by Annex III to Decision 2011/137/CFSP, using the same criteria as those listed above for the travel ban — i.e., persons engaged in acts threatening Libya's peace, stability, or security, or obstructing its democratic/political transition. [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

---

## 3. 📋 Background: Alignment with UN Security Council Resolution 2174 (2014)

The Council noted that **UNSCR 2174 (2014)** had already extended the travel ban and asset freeze to persons/entities threatening Libya's peace, stability, or security. While Annexes I and III to Decision 2011/137/CFSP had been previously updated (by Decision 2014/727/CFSP), the criteria in this 2015 Decision now **also extend to persons and entities not covered by those Annexes** — thereby giving the EU autonomous capacity to list individuals beyond those captured by the UN framework. [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

---

## 4. 🗑️ Deletion and Update of Listed Individuals (Annex)

**Annexes II and IV** to Decision 2011/137/CFSP are amended as follows: [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

- The entry concerning **Ahmed Mohammed Qadhaf Al-Dam** is **deleted** from both Annexes II and IV — following the **judgment of the General Court of 24 September 2014** in Case T-348/13 (*Kadhaf Al Dam v Council*)
- The entry concerning **one other person** is **deleted** from Annex II
- The entry concerning **one other person** in Annexes II and IV is **updated**

---

## 5. 📅 Entry into Force

The Decision entered into force on the **day following its publication** in the Official Journal of the European Union. [start_cite]32015D0382[end_cite]

---

## 🏛️ Directive 2013/58/EU — Postponement of Solvency II Dates

**CELEX ID:** `32013L0058`
**Full Title:** *Directive 2013/58/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 December 2013 amending Directive 2009/138/EC (Solvency II) as regards the date for its transposition and the date of its application, and the date of repeal of certain Directives (Solvency I)*

---

### 📌 Purpose

This Directive amends **Directive 2009/138/EC (Solvency II)** — the EU's modern, risk-based framework for the regulation and supervision of insurance and reinsurance undertakings — specifically to **postpone key implementation dates**, in order to avoid premature and burdensome legislative obligations for Member States and to allow sufficient time to prepare for the new supervisory architecture [start_cite]32013L0058[end_cite].

---

### 📅 Specific Date Changes Made

The following date amendments were made to Directive 2009/138/EC [start_cite]32013L0058[end_cite]:

| Article | Provision | Old Date | New (Postponed) Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Art. 309(1), 1st subparagraph | **Transposition deadline** | 30 June 2013 | **31 March 2015** |
| Art. 309(1), 2nd subparagraph | **Date of application** | 1 January 2014 | **1 January 2016** |
| Art. 310, 1st paragraph | **Date of application** (related) | 1 January 2014 | **1 January 2016** |
| Art. 311, 2nd paragraph | **Date of repeal of Solvency I** | 1 January 2014 | **1 January 2016** |

---

### 🔍 Key Rationale

The main driver for the postponement was the complexity of the **"Omnibus II" proposal** (Commission proposal of 19 January 2011), which — among other things — was intended to further adapt Solvency II to the new supervisory architecture (notably EIOPA) and convert certain powers into delegated/implementing acts. Given the risk that the Omnibus II proposal would not enter into force before the then-current Solvency II dates, leaving those dates unchanged would have meant Solvency II being implemented without the transitional rules and adaptations provided for by Omnibus II [start_cite]32013L0058[end_cite].

## 🚢 Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087 — Port Reception Facilities & MARPOL Annex V

**CELEX ID:** `32015L2087`
**Full Title:** *Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087 of 18 November 2015 amending Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC of the European Parliament and the Council on port reception facilities for ship-generated waste and cargo residues*

---

### 📋 Overview

This is the **2015 Transport Directive** you are looking for. It was adopted on **18 November 2015** by the European Commission and amends **Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC**, which is the principal EU legislative instrument governing port reception facilities for ship-generated waste and cargo residues. [start_cite]32015L2087[end_cite]

---

### 🔍 Why Was It Adopted?

The Directive was triggered by an international regulatory update: [start_cite]32015L2087[end_cite]

1. On **15 July 2011**, the International Maritime Organisation (**IMO**) adopted **Resolution MEPC.201(62)**, which amended **Annex V to the MARPOL Convention** on the prevention of pollution by garbage from ships. This amendment introduced a **new and more detailed categorisation of garbage**.
2. The revised MARPOL Annex V entered into force on **1 January 2013**.
3. This new categorisation was also reflected in two IMO Circulars:
   - **MEPC.1/Circ.644/Rev.1** — standard format for the *advance notification form* for waste delivery to port reception facilities.
   - **MEPC.1/Circ.645/Rev.1** — standard format for the *waste delivery receipt* following a ship's use of port reception facilities.

---

### ✅ What Does the Directive Do?

The Directive makes **two targeted changes** to Annex II of Directive 2000/59/EC: [start_cite]32015L2087[end_cite]

| Change | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Updated garbage categorisation** | The table in Annex II (covering types and amounts of waste/cargo residues to be delivered or retained on board) is adapted to match the **new garbage categories** of the amended MARPOL Annex V, ensuring consistency with IMO measures and avoiding uncertainty for port users and authorities. |
| **New waste delivery data column** | Information on the **types and quantities of ship-generated waste actually delivered to port reception facilities in the last port of delivery** is added to the Annex II table, to enable accurate calculation of sufficient storage capacity on board ships. |

---

### 🎯 Key Policy Rationale

- **Consistency with IMO:** Aligning EU law with the updated MARPOL Annex V avoids confusion for port users and authorities.
- **Improved enforcement:** Accurate delivery data from the last port supports **better-targeted ship inspections**, reducing port turnaround times.
- **Data monitoring:** Systematic collection of waste delivery data improves statistical analysis of waste flow patterns in ports and supports the information/monitoring system under Article 12(3) of Directive 2000/59/EC, linked to **SafeSeaNet** and the Port State Control database. [start_cite]32015L2087[end_cite]

---

### 📅 Transposition Deadline

Member States were required to adopt and publish the necessary implementing measures by **9 December 2016**. [start_cite]32015L2087[end_cite]

## Directive (EU) 2015/720 — Lightweight Plastic Carrier Bags

**CELEX ID:** `32015L0720`
**Full Title:** *Directive (EU) 2015/720 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2015 amending Directive 94/62/EC as regards reducing the consumption of lightweight plastic carrier bags*

---

### Background & Purpose

Directive 94/62/EC on packaging and packaging waste did not contain specific measures for plastic carrier bags. Directive (EU) 2015/720 was adopted to fill that gap, driven by concerns that [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]:

- **Littering & resource waste:** Current consumption levels result in high levels of littering and inefficient use of resources.
- **Environmental pollution:** Littering of plastic carrier bags threatens aquatic ecosystems worldwide.
- **Imbalance across Member States:** Consumption levels vary considerably across the Union; the seven best-performing Member States had already reduced consumption to only 20% of the EU average.

**"Lightweight plastic carrier bags"** are defined as bags with a **wall thickness below 50 microns**, which become waste more quickly and are more prone to littering. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]

---

### Per-Person Consumption Targets (Article 1, inserting Article 4(1a) into Directive 94/62/EC)

Member States must take measures ensuring **at least one** of the following two options:

| **Option** | **Target** | **Deadline** |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumption cap – first stage** | ≤ **90 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person** per year | **31 December 2019** |
| **Consumption cap – second stage** | ≤ **40 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person** per year | **31 December 2025** |

> *"or equivalent targets set in weight"* — Member States may use weight-based equivalents instead. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]

**Alternatively**, Member States may choose Option (b): ensure that by **31 December 2018**, lightweight plastic carrier bags are **not provided free of charge** at the point of sale, unless equally effective instruments are implemented.

> ⚠️ **Very lightweight plastic carrier bags** (wall thickness below 15 microns, used as primary packaging for loose food for hygiene purposes) **may be excluded** from national consumption objectives. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]

---

### Reporting Obligation

From **27 May 2018**, Member States are required to **report annually** on the consumption of lightweight plastic carrier bags when providing packaging data to the Commission under Article 12 of Directive 94/62/EC. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]

- **No free bags by end of 2018** unless equally effective alternatives are in place. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]
- **Labelling of biodegradable/compostable bags:** The Commission was required to adopt specifications for Union-wide labels by 27 May 2017 (new Article 8a). [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]
- **Oxo-degradable bags:** The Commission was required to report on their environmental impact by 27 May 2017. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]
- **Transposition deadline:** Member States were required to bring national implementing laws into force by **27 November 2016**. [start_cite]32015L0720[end_cite]

## Commission Directive 2013/9/EU — Amending Annex III to the Rail Interoperability Directive 2008/57/EC

### 🔍 Identification

The directive you are looking for is **Commission Directive 2013/9/EU of 11 March 2013**, with CELEX ID **32013L0009** [start_cite]32013L0009[end_cite]. It is:

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| **Document type** | Directive |
| **Year** | 2013 |
| **Policy domain** | Law |
| **Issuing body** | European Commission |

All three metadata filters are satisfied. ✅

---

### 📜 What Annex III Is About

Annex III to Directive 2008/57/EC sets out the **Essential Requirements** for the interoperability of the rail system within the Community — the foundational technical and safety standards that railway subsystems must meet [start_cite]32013L0009[end_cite].

---

### ➕ The New Concern Added: **Accessibility**

Commission Directive 2013/9/EU adds **"Accessibility"** as a new essential requirement across multiple subsystems. Specifically, Article 1 of the Directive amends Annex III in five ways [start_cite]32013L0009[end_cite]:

1. **General requirement (Section 1.6):** The **"infrastructure"** and **"rolling stock"** subsystems must be accessible to **persons with disabilities** and **persons with reduced mobility**, ensuring access on an equal basis with others through the prevention or removal of barriers. The **"operations"** and **"telematics applications for passengers"** subsystems must also provide the necessary functionality to facilitate such access.

2. **Infrastructure subsystems (Section 2.1.2):** Infrastructure subsystems to which the public has access must be accessible in accordance with the general accessibility requirement (1.6).

3. **Rolling stock subsystems (Section 2.4.5):** Rolling stock subsystems to which the public has access must be accessible in accordance with 1.6.

4. **Operations (Section 2.6.4):** Appropriate steps must be taken to ensure that operating rules provide for the functionality required to ensure accessibility.

5. **Telematics applications for passengers (Section 2.7.5):** Appropriate steps must be taken to ensure that telematics applications for passengers provide the necessary functionality to ensure accessibility.

---

### 🏛️ Why This Was Necessary

The rationale given in the Directive's recitals is threefold [start_cite]32013L0009[end_cite]:

- The **UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities** (to which the EU is a party) establishes accessibility as a general principle (Article 3) and requires States to eliminate barriers to transportation (Article 9).
- **Regulation (EC) No 1371/2007** on rail passengers' rights requires railway undertakings to ensure stations, platforms, and rolling stock are accessible via the TSI for persons with reduced mobility.
- An explicit reference to accessibility in Annex III was needed because accessibility is an **essential requirement** applying to infrastructure, rolling stock, operations, and telematics subsystems.

Member States were required to transpose the Directive by **1 January 2014** [start_cite]32013L0009[end_cite].

## 🔵 Commission Directive 2014/80/EU

**CELEX ID:** 32014L0080
**Adopted:** 20 June 2014
**Document type:** Directive (Commission)

### What it does

This Directive **amends Annex II to Directive 2006/118/EC** of the European Parliament and of the Council on the **protection of groundwater against pollution and deterioration**. [start_cite]32014L0080[end_cite]

The key reasons for the amendment, as stated in the Directive's recitals, are:

---

## 📄 Directive 2014/63/EU — Amending the EU Honey Directive

**CELEX ID:** 32014L0063
**Adopted:** 15 May 2014
**Entered into force:** 20th day following publication in the Official Journal of the EU
**Member State application deadline:** 24 June 2015

---

### 🍯 Background

Council Directive 2001/110/EC defines honey as the natural sweet substance produced by *Apis mellifera* bees, composed of different sugars, organic acids, enzymes, and **solid particles derived from honey collection**. That Directive prohibits both the addition of any ingredient (including food additives) and the removal of any constituent particular to honey — including **pollen** — unless its removal is unavoidable when extracting foreign matter. [start_cite]32014L0063[end_cite]

---

### 🔬 The Core Issue: Pollen as a "Natural Constituent"

A legal ambiguity arose around whether **pollen** should be classified as an "ingredient" of honey under Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011 on food information to consumers. That Regulation defines an "ingredient" as any substance **deliberately used** in the manufacture or preparation of a food. [start_cite]32014L0063[end_cite]

Directive 2014/63/EU clarifies that:
- Pollen enters honey naturally — via nectar collected by bees, pollen on bees' hair, pollen in the hive air, and accidental release during extraction.
- Its presence is **not** the result of any deliberate act by food business operators.
- Therefore, **pollen, being a natural constituent particular to honey, shall not be considered to be an "ingredient"** within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011. [start_cite]32014L0063[end_cite]

---

### 📌 Key Amendments Made

Directive 2014/63/EU amended Directive 2001/110/EC in several ways:

1. **Pollen is not an ingredient** — A new point was added to Article 2 of the honey directive explicitly stating this.
2. **Labelling clarification** — References to "EC honeys" were updated to "EU honeys" (e.g., "blend of EU honeys", "blend of non-EU honeys") following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon.
3. **Commission powers revised** — The power to adopt **delegated acts** was conferred on the Commission to lay down quantitative parameters for the "mainly" criterion (floral/vegetable origin) and minimal pollen content in filtered honey. The previously wider power to align provisions with general EU food legislation was deleted as no longer necessary.
4. **Comitology updated** — Provisions were aligned to Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 on implementing powers. [start_cite]32014L0063[end_cite]

---

### ⚠️ Important Caveat: Genetically Modified Pollen

The Directive explicitly states that it is **without prejudice** to Regulation (EC) No 1829/2003 on genetically modified food. Honey containing **genetically modified pollen** is still regarded as "food produced from a GMO" and remains subject to authorisation, supervision, and, where applicable, labelling requirements under that Regulation. [start_cite]32014L0063[end_cite]

---

rows.question: Which EU regulation codifies the common rules for exports of goods from the Union?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015R0479 - Regulation (EU) 2015/479 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 March 2015 on common rules for exports (codification).
rows.actualResponse:
The search returned a directly responsive document. Here is a comprehensive answer:

---

## EU Regulation on Common Rules for Exports

The EU regulation that codifies the common rules for exports of goods from the Union is:

> **Regulation (EU) 2015/479 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 March 2015 on common rules for exports (codification)**
> **CELEX ID: 32015R0479**

---

### Background & Purpose

[start_cite]32015R0479[end_cite] was adopted as a **codification** of the previously substantially amended Council Regulation (EC) No 1061/2009. In the interests of clarity and rationality, the earlier regulation was fully replaced and consolidated into this single instrument. The legal basis is **Article 207(2) TFEU** (common commercial policy).

---

### Core Principle

The regulation's fundamental rule, set out in **Chapter I (Article 1)**, is:

> *"The exportation of products from the Union to third countries shall be free, that is to say, they shall not be subject to any quantitative restriction, with the exception of those restrictions which are applied in conformity with this Regulation."*

This reflects the broader principle that **exports are almost completely liberalised** across all Member States. [start_cite]32015R0479[end_cite]

---

### Key Provisions

The regulation is structured around four main chapters:

| Chapter | Content |
|---|---|
| **I** | Basic Principle – free exportation as the rule |
| **II** | Union Information & Consultation Procedure – Member States must notify the Commission of unusual market developments; the Commission may request export surveillance data |
| **III** | Protective Measures – the Commission may require **export authorisations** or impose **quantitative restrictions** to prevent critical shortages of essential products, or to fulfil international obligations |
| **IV** | Transitional & Final Provisions – special rules for oil products allocation systems, reporting obligations, safeguards for public policy/security/health, and relationship to agricultural market instruments |

---

### Protective Measures Mechanism

When **Union interests call for immediate intervention** (e.g., shortage of essential products), the Commission may — acting on a Member State's request or on its own initiative — make exports subject to an **export authorisation** (Article 5). More lasting protective measures can be adopted via the **examination procedure** before the Committee on Safeguards (Article 3 & 6). [start_cite]32015R0479[end_cite]

---

### Scope

The regulation applies to **all products**, whether **industrial or agricultural**, but operates as a complement to instruments establishing the common organisation of agricultural markets. It does not preclude Member States from applying quantitative export restrictions on grounds of public morality, public policy, public security, or the protection of health and life. [start_cite]32015R0479[end_cite]

---

## ✅ Identified Directive: Council Directive (EU) 2015/121

**CELEX ID:** `32015L0121`
**Full Title:** *Council Directive (EU) 2015/121 of 27 January 2015 amending Directive 2011/96/EU on the common system of taxation applicable in the case of parent companies and subsidiaries of different Member States*

---

### 📌 Summary & Key Provisions

This is a short but highly targeted directive adopted by **the Council of the European Union on 27 January 2015**, amending the Parent-Subsidiary Directive (2011/96/EU).

#### Why Was It Needed?
[start_cite]32015L0121[end_cite] The original Directive 2011/96/EU exempts dividends and other profit distributions paid by subsidiary companies to their parent companies from withholding taxes and eliminates double taxation at the level of the parent company. However, some Member States had no domestic or agreement-based provisions to prevent abuse, and those that did had provisions of varying severity. The inclusion of a **common minimum anti-abuse rule** was deemed necessary to prevent misuse and ensure greater consistency across Member States.

#### The Anti-Abuse Rule Introduced
[start_cite]32015L0121[end_cite] Article 1 of the Directive replaces Article 1(2) of Directive 2011/96/EU with the following key provisions:

- **New Article 1(2):** Member States shall **not grant the benefits** of the Directive to an arrangement or a series of arrangements that are **not genuine**, having been put in place for the **main purpose (or one of the main purposes) of obtaining a tax advantage** that defeats the object or purpose of the Directive.
- **New Article 1(3):** An arrangement is regarded as **not genuine** to the extent it is not put in place for **valid commercial reasons which reflect economic reality**.
- **New Article 1(4):** The Directive does not preclude the application of domestic or agreement-based provisions required for the prevention of **tax evasion, tax fraud or abuse**.

#### Proportionality & the "To-the-Extent Approach"
[start_cite]32015L0121[end_cite] The application of anti-abuse rules must be **proportionate** and target arrangements that do not reflect economic reality. Member States may also apply the anti-abuse clause to tackle specific steps or parts of an arrangement on a stand-alone basis (*the "to the extent approach"*), without invalidating the remaining genuine parts.

#### Transposition Deadline
[start_cite]32015L0121[end_cite] Member States were required to bring into force the necessary laws, regulations, and administrative provisions to comply with this Directive by **31 December 2015** at the latest.

---

## ✈️ Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 — Aviation ETS "Stop the Clock"

**CELEX ID:** `32014R0421`
**Title:** *Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 April 2014 amending Directive 2003/87/EC establishing a scheme for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading within the Community, in view of the implementation by 2020 of an international agreement applying a single global market-based measure to international aviation emissions*

---

|---|---|
| **Document type** | Regulation ✅ |
| **Year** | 2014 ✅ |
| **Policy domain** | Transport ✅ |
| **Adopted** | 16 April 2014 |
| **Adopted by** | European Parliament and the Council |

---

### 🔍 What the Regulation Does

**Regulation (EU) No 421/2014** introduces a **temporary derogation** from Directive 2003/87/EC (the EU ETS Directive) for aviation emissions — commonly referred to as the **"stop the clock"** approach. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

#### 1. Core Derogation (the "Stop the Clock")
The Regulation inserts a new **Article 28a** into Directive 2003/87/EC. Under this provision, Member States are required to treat ETS monitoring, reporting and surrender obligations as **satisfied** — and to take **no enforcement action** — in respect of: [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

- **All emissions from flights to and from aerodromes located in countries outside the EEA**, for the period **1 January 2013 to 31 December 2016**.
- Flights between an aerodrome in an **outermost region** of the EU (within the meaning of Article 349 TFEU) and an aerodrome in **another EEA region**, for the same period.

In practical terms, this **limits the EU ETS aviation coverage exclusively to intra-EEA flights** during that window — only routes between aerodromes within the EEA remain subject to the full ETS obligations. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

#### 2. Rationale: ICAO Negotiations
The derogation was introduced to **sustain momentum from the 38th ICAO Assembly (October 2013)** and to facilitate progress at the upcoming 39th Session in 2016, where the EU aimed to secure a **global market-based measure (MBM)** for international aviation emissions. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

#### 3. Adjusted Allowances and Timelines
- Free allowances and auctioned allowances were **reduced proportionately** to reflect the reduced surrender obligation.
- The **surrender and reporting deadlines for 2013 emissions** were extended: verified 2013 emissions from intra-EEA flights had to be reported by **31 March 2015** (instead of 2014) and surrendered by **30 April 2015** (instead of 2014). [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

#### 4. Small Emitters
- **Non-commercial aircraft operators emitting less than 1,000 tonnes CO₂ per year** are fully exempted from Annex I of Directive 2003/87/EC for the period **1 January 2013 to 31 December 2020**.
- Aircraft operators with **annual emissions below 25,000 tonnes CO₂** may use a simplified **Eurocontrol "small emitters tool"** for verification, reducing the administrative burden. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

#### 5. Post-2016 Review
The Commission was required to report to the European Parliament and Council **after the 2016 ICAO Assembly** on the scope of coverage for emissions from flights outside the EEA from 1 January 2017 onwards, with the possibility of new proposals. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

---

### 🔑 Key Features

The Directive lays down rules on **calculation methods and reporting requirements** in accordance with Directive 98/70/EC. It applies to: [start_cite]32015L0652[end_cite]
- Fuels used to propel **road vehicles**
- **Non-road mobile machinery** (including inland waterway vessels when not at sea)

Member States must ensure that **fuel suppliers** use the calculation method set out in **Annex I** to determine the greenhouse-gas intensity of the fuels they supply, for the purposes of Article 7a(2) of Directive 98/70/EC. [start_cite]32015L0652[end_cite]

Key design choices include:
- The method is based on **average greenhouse-gas intensities** representing an industry-average value typical for a particular fuel, thereby reducing the administrative burden on suppliers and Member States.

- A **simplified method** in Annex I is to be applied for **SME suppliers**.

## Regulation (EU) 2015/476 — Union Measures Following a WTO DSB Report (Codification)

| **Document Type** | Regulation |

---

### 📌 Background & Purpose

This Regulation is a **codification** of Council Regulation (EC) No 1515/2001, which had been substantially amended. In the interests of clarity and rationality, the original regulation was codified into this single, consolidated instrument. [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

It was adopted under **Article 207(2) TFEU** (the Common Commercial Policy legal basis), following the ordinary legislative procedure. [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

---

### ⚖️ Core Legal Framework

The Regulation operates within the WTO framework established by the **Understanding on Rules and Procedures Governing the Settlement of Disputes (DSU)**, under which the **Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** was established pursuant to the Marrakesh Agreement. [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

It connects specifically to two pre-existing EU trade defence instruments:
- **Regulation (EC) No 1225/2009** — on protection against dumped imports
- **Regulation (EC) No 597/2009** — on protection against subsidised imports

---

### 🔧 Key Measures Available to the Union (Article 1)

Whenever the DSB adopts a report concerning a Union measure taken under Regulation (EC) No 1225/2009, No 597/2009, or the Regulation itself (a **"disputed measure"**), the **Commission** may take one or more of the following actions: [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

1. **Repeal or amend** the disputed measure; or
2. **Adopt any other special implementing measure** deemed appropriate to bring the Union into conformity with the DSB report's recommendations and rulings.

Additionally, the Commission may: [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]
- **Request information** from interested parties to supplement the investigation record.
- **Initiate a review** before or simultaneously with taking action under Article 1.
- **Suspend** the disputed or amended measure for a **limited period** (using the advisory procedure).

---

### 🔧 Non-Disputed Measures (Article 2)

The Commission may also take any of the measures described above regarding a **non-disputed measure**, if it considers this appropriate in light of legal interpretations made in a DSB report. [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

---

### 📅 Temporal Effect (Article 3)

Any measures adopted under this Regulation take effect **from the date of their entry into force** and **do not provide a basis for reimbursement** of duties collected prior to that date, unless otherwise provided. This reflects the principle that DSB recommendations have **only prospective effect**. [start_cite]32015R0476[end_cite]

---

### 🏛️ Committee Procedure (Article 4)

Implementation is assisted by the Committee established under Regulation (EC) No 1225/2009, acting under **Regulation (EU) No 182/2011**:
- **Advisory procedure** → for suspension of measures (Article 4(2))
- **Examination procedure** → for all other measures (Article 4(3))

## Council Directive 2013/51/Euratom — Radioactive Substances in Drinking Water

### Identification

The directive you are looking for is **COUNCIL DIRECTIVE 2013/51/EURATOM**, adopted on **22 October 2013**, with CELEX ID **32013L0051**. It satisfies all three metadata filters: year 2013, document type Directive, and policy domain Science. [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

---

### Purpose and Legal Basis

The Directive was adopted by the Council of the EU on the basis of **Articles 31 and 32 of the Treaty establishing the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom Treaty)**. Its stated subject matter is to:

> *"lay down requirements for the protection of the health of the general public with regard to radioactive substances in water intended for human consumption. It lays down parametric values and frequencies and methods for monitoring radioactive substances."* [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

The Directive acknowledges that **ingestion of water is one of the pathways of incorporation of radioactive substances into the human body**, and that the existing Directive 98/83/EC on drinking water quality contained indicator parameters for radioactive substances, but those fell within the scope of the basic standards under the Euratom Treaty. As a result, this Directive **supersedes** Directive 98/83/EC with respect to radioactive substances specifically. [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

---

### Radionuclides Brought into Scope

The Directive explicitly brings the following radionuclides into its scope:

#### 1. 🔬 **Radon** (radon-222) and Its Decay Products
The recitals note that:
> *"Taking into consideration the large geographical variability in the natural occurrence of radon, the Commission adopted Recommendation 2001/928/Euratom, which deals with the quality of water intended for human consumption regarding radon and long-lived radon decay products. **It is appropriate to include these radionuclides in the scope of this Directive.**"* [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

Covered decay products include:
- **Short-lived radon decay products** — given their own monitoring treatment and excluded from the Indicative Dose calculation.
- **Long-lived radon decay products** — explicitly incorporated from the prior Recommendation.

#### 2. ⚗️ **Tritium** (Hydrogen-3)
Tritium is explicitly identified as a **separate parametric value** subject to its own monitoring. It is **excluded from the Indicative Dose (ID)** calculation, meaning it must be monitored independently. The Directive notes that Member States required to monitor for tritium shall carry out sampling and analysis **at least once per year**. [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

#### 3. 📊 **All Other Radionuclides (via the Indicative Dose)**
The Directive defines the **"Indicative Dose" (ID)** as:
> *"the committed effective dose for one year of ingestion resulting from **all the radionuclides whose presence has been detected** in a supply of water intended for human consumption, of **natural and artificial origin**, but **excluding tritium, potassium-40, radon and short-lived radon decay products**."* [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

This means all detected radionuclides — both naturally occurring and of artificial/man-made origin — are captured under the ID parametric value, except those excluded above.

#### ❌ Excluded from the Indicative Dose (but still mentioned)
- **Potassium-40** — excluded from the ID calculation (not separately parameterised within the Directive's scope).
- Tritium, radon, and short-lived decay products (as noted above, these have their own separate treatment).

---

### Key Structural Elements

| Radionuclide / Group | How Addressed |
|---|---|
| **Radon** (radon-222) | Separate parametric value, Annex I; monitoring per Annex II |
| **Short-lived radon decay products** | Excluded from ID; monitored separately |
| **Long-lived radon decay products** | Included in scope via Annex I |
| **Tritium** | Separate parametric value; at least annual monitoring |
| **All other radionuclides** (natural & artificial) | Covered under the **Indicative Dose** parametric value |
| **Potassium-40** | Excluded from the ID calculation |

---

### Scope Exemptions

The Directive **does not apply** to:
- **Natural mineral waters** (governed by Directive 2009/54/EC), and
- **Waters that are medicinal products** (governed by Directive 2001/83/EC). [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

Member States may also exempt small individual supplies providing fewer than 10 m³/day or serving fewer than 50 persons (unless part of a commercial/public activity). [start_cite]32013L0051[end_cite]

I have retrieved and read the full text of Regulation (EU) No 421/2014. Here is a complete and accurate answer based solely on that document's content.

---

## Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 — Aviation & Third-Country Flights: No Per-Tonne CO₂ Price

**CELEX ID:** 32014R0421 — **REGULATION (EU) No 421/2014 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of 16 April 2014 amending Directive 2003/87/EC (the EU Emissions Trading System), *"in view of the implementation by 2020 of an international agreement applying a single global market-based measure to international aviation emissions."* [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

---

### What the Regulation Actually Does for Flights to/from Third Countries

**Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 does not set any fixed per-tonne CO₂ price for flights to and from third countries.** The EU ETS is a market-based trading scheme where allowance prices are determined by the carbon market, not legislated by the Regulation itself.

What the Regulation *does* establish is a **temporary derogation** — essentially a suspension of EU ETS obligations — for those flights: [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

> *"Member States shall consider the requirements set out in those provisions to be satisfied and shall take no action against aircraft operators in respect of: **all emissions from flights to and from aerodromes located in countries outside the European Economic Area (EEA)** in each calendar year from **1 January 2013 to 31 December 2016**."*

---

### Key Provisions Regarding Third-Country Flights

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Scope of derogation** | All flights to/from aerodromes in countries **outside the EEA** |
| **Period covered** | 1 January 2013 – 31 December 2016 |
| **Effect on obligations** | Aircraft operators are **not required** to monitor, report, or surrender allowances for emissions from those routes during this period |
| **Free allowances** | Reduced proportionally; unallocated allowances are **cancelled**, not traded [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite] |
| **Auctions** | Member States auction a proportionally **reduced** number of allowances for the same period [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite] |
| **Small emitters** | Operators with total annual emissions below **25,000 tonnes CO₂** may use a simplified verification tool (Eurocontrol's small emitters tool) [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite] |
| **Non-commercial small operators** | Non-commercial operators emitting less than **1,000 tonnes CO₂ per year** are exempt from Annex I of Directive 2003/87/EC from 2013 to 2020 [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite] |

---

### Why No Price Is Set

The Regulation's explicit purpose is to **suspend** — not price — the ETS obligation for international routes, in order to support progress at the ICAO 38th Assembly (2013) and the development of a **global market-based measure (MBM)** for international aviation. The carbon price in the EU ETS is always market-determined through allowance trading, and this Regulation does not override that mechanism. [start_cite]32014R0421[end_cite]

---

### ⚠️ Evidence Assessment

No retrieved document (including the Regulation itself) contains any fixed per-tonne CO₂ price applicable to flights to and from third countries. The premise of the question — that the Regulation sets such a price — is not supported by the document text.

### ⚠️ No Per-Member-State Euro Budget Allocations Exist in This Document

After carefully reading the full text of **32013D1386**, I must report plainly: **the 7th EAP Decision does not allocate any specific euro amounts to individual Member States.**

This is because Decision 1386/2013/EU is a **framework programme decision**, not a financial regulation or appropriations act. Its operative provisions are:

- **Article 1** — Formally adopts the 7th EAP (set out in the Annex) for the period up to 31 December 2020.
- **Article 2** — Establishes nine **priority objectives** (e.g., protecting natural capital, transitioning to a low-carbon economy, improving implementation of environment law, securing investment for environment and climate policy, etc.).
- **Article 3** — Places responsibility on **Union institutions and Member States** to take appropriate action, respecting the principles of conferral, subsidiarity, and proportionality.
- **Article 4** — Requires the Commission to **monitor** implementation in the context of the Europe 2020 Strategy, carry out an evaluation, and if appropriate propose an 8th EAP.
- **Article 5** — Entry into force.

Recital (28) of the Decision acknowledges that *"environment and climate objectives should be supported by adequate investments, and funds should be spent more effectively"*, and Recital (29) refers to environmental integration across policy areas. However, **no specific financial envelope or per-country allocations are defined in the decision text itself**. [start_cite]32013D1386[end_cite]

Financing for actions under the 7th EAP is meant to flow through **separate pre-existing EU funding instruments** (such as the LIFE programme, Structural Funds, the Cohesion Fund, etc.), each governed by their own distinct legal acts — not by this Decision.
